name: basic-turn
description: Smallest possible round-trip. Verifies session detection and a clean ready→working→ready cycle.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

### agent_supports: yes

Aider supports a non-interactive, single-shot turn via the `--message`/`-m` flag: it processes one natural-language instruction, applies any edits, and exits — no interactive chat session. See https://aider.chat/docs/scripting.html. This is exactly the `headless_mode` capability the `basic-turn` primitive requires (`replaydata/agents/aider/capabilities.json` → `headless_mode: true`). The canonical recipe drives aider interactively in a tmux REPL (a drift fix: a headless `--message` run exits before the IdleFlush settle fires), but the primitive being exercised — transcript turn-end detection — still anchors on `headless_mode`.

The `basic-turn` recipe prompt — "Reply with exactly the word: ok" — is a pure one-message round-trip with no tool use (`tool_calls_max: 0`), which is the smallest turn aider can run. Aider persists every exchange to `.aider.chat.history.md` in the project CWD (https://aider.chat/docs/faq.html) and prints a `> Tokens: X sent, Y received.` line after each model call (e.g. `Tokens: 7.7k sent, 167 received`), confirmed across the aider docs and HISTORY. So a clean single turn produces a fully-formed transcript with real token usage.

### daemon_capability: full

The aider adapter is `FilesUnderCWD{Filename: ".aider.chat.history.md", Parser: RawLineParser}` (`core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/agent.go`). Walking the two User-observable signals against the parser as it exists today:

1. **`ready → working → ready`** — observed correctly. The `#### ` user-prompt line emits a `user_message` event (`parser.go` `ParseLineRaw`). The SessionDetector force-bounces a ready session to working when fresh activity arrives with a non-empty `LastEventType` (`session_detector_activity.go:343-350`, `ForceReadyToWorkingReason`), so working is entered. Aider's idle TUI prompt never lands in the markdown history, so there is no in-band turn-end marker; the parser synthesizes `turn_done` out-of-band via `IdleFlush` once the transcript has been quiet for `aiderIdleTurnDoneAfter` (1500ms). `turn_done` is the authoritative end-of-turn signal (`session.go` `IsAgentDone`), flipping the session back to ready. The final state is `ready` and the topology is exactly `ready → working → ready`.
2. **`TotalTokens > 0`** — observed correctly. The `> Tokens:` line is matched by `tokensRE` and routed through `closeModelCall`, which emits an `assistant_message` carrying a `TokenSnapshot{Input, Output, Total}`. `applyTokenSnapshot` (`tailer_metrics.go:119-125`) sets `metrics.TotalTokens = tokens.Total` whenever `Total > 0`. For the recipe's `> Tokens: 771 sent, 1 received.` this yields `TotalTokens = 772`.

Both spec-required observables (the topology and the non-zero token count, per `scenarios.json → verify`) are emitted correctly by the parser today, so the daemon is `full`. The one wrinkle — the working→ready edge is synthesized from quiescence rather than an in-band marker, and lands with ~5s effective latency (1500ms IdleFlush floor clamped to the SessionDetector's 5s working-refresh poll, documented at `parser.go:42-54`) — affects only TIMING, not state correctness. Latency is not a `verify` assertion, so it is a caveat, not a `bug`: there is no spec-required observable the daemon mis-handles, and the trace fully exists in the watched Source.

### driver_capability: ready

The interactive driver (`scripts/drive-aider-interactive.sh`) implements `send`, `wait_turn`, `slash`, `interrupt`, and `sleep`. The `basic-turn` recipe is the canonical happy path: one `send` ("Reply with exactly the word: ok"), one `wait_turn` (blocks on the next `> Tokens:` line), then an 8s `sleep` to cover the IdleFlush + working-refresh settle before teardown. Every step type the recipe needs is implemented, so the driver is `ready` — no gapped primitive (restart/reset_session/resume/start_session/exit_clean/sigkill/keys/session/fork) is touched by this recipe.
caveats: feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant: aider's idle TUI prompt is never written to .aider.chat.history.md, so there is no in-band turn-end marker. The parser synthesizes turn_done via IdleFlush instead — the state arc is correct, but the end-of-turn is inferred from quiescence rather than observed.

metric-drift-downstream: the working→ready transition lands with ~5s effective latency (1500ms IdleFlush floor clamped to the SessionDetector's 5s working-refresh poll), so the session shows as working for several seconds after aider has finished. State correctness is unaffected; only the transition timing is delayed.
The recipe uses the same trailing-sleep settle as the sibling full-lifecycle-toolcall cell (>=8s) so the synthesized working→ready edge lands before the tmux kill-session teardown.

sources.note: --message/-m one-shot non-interactive mode: process one instruction, apply edits, exit — the headless_mode capability the primitive requires

aider prints '> Tokens: X sent, Y received.' per model call; total tokens sent/received tracked
Source = FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser} — per-project markdown transcript; no PendingContributor/SubagentCounter

#### → user_message; > Tokens: → assistant_message w/ TokenSnapshot; no in-band turn-end → IdleFlush synthesizes turn_done (1500ms floor at lines 42-54); ~5s effective working→ready latency documented there — proves the latency caveat, not a bug
applyTokenSnapshot (lines 119-125): metrics.TotalTokens = tokens.Total when Total > 0 — emits the TotalTokens>0 signal correctly (daemon_capability:full)
IsAgentDone: LastEventType == turn_done is the authoritative end-of-turn → flips working to ready
lines 343-350: force ready→working on fresh activity so ClassifyState can later detect the working→ready edge
TestParser_BaselineHello_FullTurn (lines 35-118): uses the exact basic-turn recipe prompt; pins assistant_message+TokenSnapshot on > Tokens: and turn_done from IdleFlush
driver implements send/wait_turn/slash/interrupt/sleep — basic-turn needs only send + wait_turn + sleep, all present → driver_capability:ready
headless_mode: true — the Primitive exercised maps to this capability key (confirmed)

preconditions: aider CLI on PATH and authenticated against its configured provider/model (auth is set up out-of-band; the rig's precheck delegates auth to the user)
tmux available — the interactive driver runs aider in a tmux REPL so it stays alive across the turn
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver (drive-aider-interactive.sh) git-inits a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, launches `aider --no-auto-commits --yes-always --no-gitignore` in a tmux session, and waits for the `> Repo-map:` banner line in .aider.chat.history.md before sending the prompt

aider stores its transcript as .aider.chat.history.md in the cwd (FilesUnderCWD), so the daemon keys the whole session by proc-<PID> — there is no separate transcript-UUID rollout session; the turn arc, pid_discovered and teardown all land on that one proc-<PID> row

WHY interactive, not headless `aider --message` (drift fix, same shape as the sibling full-lifecycle-toolcall + pi cells): aider emits no end-of-turn marker, so working→ready is synthesized by IdleFlush after a ~1.5s quiet window, clamped to the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll; a headless --message run exits ~2.6s after the working flip — BEFORE that settle fires — so the daemon never observes the terminal working→ready edge and turn_end + teardown can't anchor (the prior 3/5 result). Keeping aider alive in the REPL across one send + wait_turn lets the daemon observe the full ready→working→ready settle cleanly, and the single tmux kill-session teardown then yields one clean process_exited

wait_turn blocks until a new `> Tokens:` line appears in the transcript (aider's per-round close marker), so the trailing sleep never races a still-running turn; the 8s trailing sleep covers aider's ~1.5s IdleFlush quiet window plus the 5s working-refresh poll (one full idle-flush cycle plus slack) so the working→ready settle is observed before teardown

The send is a no-tool single turn — aider is asked to reply with exactly one word, so the working span carries no auto-executed tool call (tool_calls_max: 0) and the session never enters waiting (aider has no permission hook)

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new + presession_created for the proc-<PID> session born ready within ~1s of REPL launch
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the same proc-<PID> session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the proc-<PID> session_id when the prompt is sent (the single assistant turn)

events.jsonl contains a state_transition→ready on the same proc-<PID> session_id once aider's turn-end is inferred (the `> Tokens:` line closes the model call and IdleFlush synthesizes turn_done after ~1.5s of quiescence, clamped to the 5s working-refresh poll) — observed within the 8s trailing sleep before teardown
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting for the session at any point (no tool call, no permission gate)
the session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the proc-<PID> session at REPL teardown

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

The spec's two Expected: bullets are the foundational lifecycle assertion that every other scenario builds on:

1. **`ready → working → ready`.** Standard turn arc. Observable in irrlicht via `state_transition` events emitted by the claudecode parser: the user-line append in the JSONL transcript drives `ready → working`; the assistant's terminal `stop_reason: end_turn` drives `working → ready`. Both transitions are first-class in `core/adapters/inbound/agents/claudecode/parser.go` and have been load-bearing since the adapter's initial commit.
2. **`TotalTokens > 0` after the turn.** Observable in the recording's `events.jsonl` via the parser's `extractAnthropicUsageBreakdown` helper, which pulls `input_tokens` / `output_tokens` / `cache_*` from each assistant message's `usage` payload. The spec assertion isn't expressible in the current phase DSL (no metric-form invariant), but the data IS in the recording — committed manifest carries the token totals.

The recipe is the minimum-cost prompt that exercises this arc end-to-end: `'Reply with exactly the word: ok'`. Single user line, single assistant line with `stop_reason: end_turn`, no tool calls, no waiting state. The 4/4 passing committed recording (`recordings/2026-05-15T07-37_irrlichd-0.4.x/`) demonstrates the spec lands cleanly across re-records.

This is the canonical baseline cell for every other claudecode scenario — failure here would invalidate dozens of derived recipes.

## What irrlicht does NOT see (and why that's narrow)

The `TotalTokens > 0` bullet from the spec doesn't have a corresponding phase DSL field; it's verified by reading the recording's metrics directly. Bringing it under the validator would require a `min_total_tokens` invariant or similar — future work for the phase DSL, not a transport gap.
caveats: Assessment backfilled in 2026-05-18 to fill the Stage-1 artifact gap. The recording + spec predate the assessment.json convention (introduced in commit de926ab); the verdict here is consistent with the existing 4/4 pass-rate and the committed recipe in scenarios.json.
TotalTokens > 0 assertion lives outside the phase DSL; it's structurally verified by the manifest's recorded metrics rather than by expected-validate.

sources.note: extractAnthropicUsageBreakdown + end_turn → ready classifier path; load-bearing since the adapter's initial commit.
Feature: Basic turn (L112-119) — two Expected: bullets (state arc + tokens > 0).
by_adapter.claudecode for basic-turn — single-prompt headless recipe.

recipe.prompt: Reply with exactly the word: ok

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

`basic-turn` is the smallest possible round-trip: one user message, one assistant reply, no tool use, asserting the `ready -> working -> ready` arc with `TotalTokens > 0` after the turn. Its primitive is transcript turn-end detection and it requires `headless_mode`.

**agent_supports: yes.** Codex CLI 0.130.0 runs a single prompt non-interactively via its `exec` headless mode, which is recorded in the codex adapter's `capabilities.json` as `headless_mode: true` and `structured_output_jsonl: true`. The committed codex regression fixture `regression/baseline-hello` is itself a captured headless single-turn round-trip, demonstrating the agent completes a basic turn and writes the JSONL transcript the daemon tails.

**daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready.** The codex adapter watches session transcripts via `FilesUnderRoot{Dir: sessionsDir(), Parser: JSONLineParser{...}}` (agent.go) and the JSONL `Parser` (parser.go) translates the `task_complete` event_msg into an `EventType: "turn_done"`, so `IsAgentDone()` fires through the primary path and drives the `working -> ready` transition. The same parser extracts per-turn and cumulative token usage from `token_count` events (`last_token_usage` / `total_token_usage`), yielding the non-zero `TotalTokens` the spec asserts. Parser unit tests `TestParser_FullTranscript_EndDetection`, `TestParser_TurnCompleteEmitsTurnDone`, and `TestParser_MultiTurnTokenCount_UsesPerTurnSnapshot` cover exactly this turn-end + token-accounting behavior. The existing coverage matrix already records codex/basic-turn as yes/yes; this assessment ratifies it.

No carve-outs apply: codex assigns turn boundaries cleanly via `task_complete` (unlike aider, whose idle-flush turn-end carries ~5s latency and is recorded as a `partial` in the matrix).

TestParser_FullTranscript_EndDetection, TestParser_TurnCompleteEmitsTurnDone, TestParser_MultiTurnTokenCount_UsesPerTurnSnapshot

irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver launches `codex --no-alt-screen` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits for the TUI banner + MCP boot before sending the prompt
Each send bumps the expected-turn counter; wait_turn blocks until a new event_msg/task_complete line appears in the rollout, so the trailing sleep never races a still-running turn

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a presession_created (proc-<PID>) event in ready within 1000 ms of agent launch
events.jsonl contains a transcript_new for the rollout session with a distinct session_id, born ready
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the rollout session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the rollout session_id when the prompt is sent (the single assistant turn)
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→ready on the same rollout session_id once the turn completes (the interactive REPL returns to ready, unlike headless codex exec which exits)
the rollout session's final live state is ready before the driver tears the REPL down
events.jsonl contains a process_exited on the rollout session at REPL teardown
no state_transition to waiting appears for the rollout session at any point

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

basic-turn asserts the smallest round-trip: a single plain-text user message, one assistant reply, no tool use, producing the `ready → working → ready` arc with `TotalTokens > 0`.

OpenCode supports a true headless turn: `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` accepts the prompt directly and blocks until the assistant turn completes. Session state lives in OpenCode's SQLite store (`~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db`), which the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls for the UUID-keyed `ses_…` session.

The daemon observes the full turn arc. The opencode parser maps OpenCode's `step-finish` part with `reason="stop"` to a `turn_done` event that closes the turn (parser.go: `case "stop": ev.EventType = "turn_done"`), driving the session back to `ready`. The committed sibling recording (`long-idle-live-session/events.jsonl`) confirms a single `opencode run` produces exactly the spec arc on one `ses_…` session: `new_state=ready` (new session created) → `working` (force ready→working on first activity) → `ready` (agent finished turn → ready). Token/cost data from step-finish populates a PerTurnContribution, so TotalTokens is non-zero.

The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) launches a blocking `opencode run` per `send` and chains `--session` for continuity; `wait_turn` is a no-op because the run already blocks. basic-turn is a single plain-text turn (one `send` + `wait_turn` + trailing `sleep`) — well within this grammar, so `driver_capability: ready`.

MODEL note: the column reassessment confirms opencode's default model is a local LM Studio model (`lmstudio/gemma-4-e2b-it-uncensored`); the driver runs `opencode run` with no `--model` flag and uses that default. The model is small and slow (~30–45s/turn), so the recipe budgets generous timeouts.
caveats: OpenCode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs; the driver discovers the session id by querying SQLite for the most recent row keyed on directory after the first `opencode run` returns.
The local gemma model is slow (~30–45s per turn); the recipe uses a 180s timeout so a slow turn does not trip a false timeout.

sources.note: parseStepFinish: reason="stop" → EventType "turn_done" closes the turn (working→ready); step-finish token/cost data builds a PerTurnContribution for all reasons except mid-turn tool-calls, so TotalTokens > 0.
Source variant ProcessOwnedStore (SQLite at ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db) with ExactName{Name: "opencode"} process matcher; daemon polls the session table for the ses_… session.
Sibling recording: a single `opencode run` emits state_transition new_state ready→working→ready on one ses_… session — the exact basic-turn arc.
`opencode run` accepts a prompt directly and runs non-interactively (headless), blocking until the turn completes.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated against its default model (run a single message in the opencode TUI to confirm; the default is a local LM Studio model so no remote key is needed)
irrlichd is recording with the opencode SQLite watcher enabled — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record
setup: The interactive driver (drive-opencode-interactive.sh) creates a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ and launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` for the single send (no --model flag, so opencode uses its configured default model); the run blocks until the assistant turn finishes, so wait_turn is a no-op

OpenCode session state lives in its SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db), not per-cwd transcript files; the daemon's ProcessOwnedStore source polls the session table and observes the UUID-keyed ses_… session for this cwd. The driver discovers that session id from SQLite after the run returns (opencode does not expose CLI-assignable session IDs)

The send is a no-tool single turn — opencode is asked to reply with exactly one word, so the working span carries no auto-executed tool call (tool_calls_max: 0) and the session never enters waiting (no permission gate fires)

The default model is a small, slow local LM Studio model (~30–45s per turn), so the trailing sleep and timeout are generous: a slow turn must not trip a false timeout, and the trailing sleep must outlast the turn_done settle so working→ready is observed

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new (or session_created) for the ses_… session, born ready, within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the ses_… session_id when the prompt is sent (the single assistant turn)
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→ready on the same ses_… session_id once the turn completes (step-finish reason=stop → turn_done closes the turn)
no state_transition to waiting appears for the session at any point (no tool call, no permission gate)
the session's final live state is ready

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

The scenario's User-observable signals are (1) the `ready → working → ready` state arc and (2) `TotalTokens > 0` after the turn. Pi supports the smallest possible round-trip natively: a single `pi --print -p <prompt>` invocation runs one assistant turn with no tool use and exits. Pi writes one JSONL session file per invocation under `~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/<ts>_<UUID>.jsonl` immediately on launch (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy), so the daemon observes a `proc-<PID>` pre-session born ready, then a `transcript_new` + `state_transition`→ready for the UUID-keyed rollout session, then the working→ready flip as the single turn runs and completes, then `process_exited` at headless exit.

The pi parser identifies the assistant message end as a complete turn boundary: `stopReason=stop` on the final assistant event maps to `turn_done`, settling working→ready. This is the same turn-end detection the sibling session-start cell already exercises for the launch arc; basic-turn adds the working→ready turn on the rollout session. `TotalTokens > 0` is covered by the parser's per-turn token snapshot (not expressible in the phase DSL today; covered by parser unit tests).
caveats: Pi cannot accept a pre-assigned UUID — it generates one itself. The headless driver discovers the UUID from the session header on line 1 of the freshly-created transcript.

`pi --print` exits after the single turn, so the rollout session's terminal working→ready settle and the process_exited can land close together; the spec anchors turn_end to turn_start (not to session_birth) so the matcher picks the post-turn ready, not the earlier birth ready.

sources.note: Identity.Name=pi, Source variant FilesUnderRoot under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/, ExactName{Name: "pi"} process matcher.
stopReason=stop on the final assistant event maps to turn_done (working→ready).
Headless `pi --print -p <prompt>` single-shot driver; resolves the freshly-created transcript and extracts the UUID from line 1.

recipe.prompt: Reply with exactly the word: ok
